does: This paper develops a modular framework for decomposing sabotage behaviors into component skills and uses synthetic simulations to train and strengthen adversarial attacks in complex environments. Rather than treating sabotage as a monolithic objective, the approach breaks it down into constituent capabilities that can be individually learned and combined. The researchers leverage synthetic data generation to augment red team attacks, allowing them to test and improve adversarial strategies in controlled simulated settings before deployment against real systems. The framework provides a structured method for systematically strengthening red team evaluations by identifying critical sub-skills and optimizing them through simulation.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of AI safety through red teaming and adversarial evaluation, extending methods that treat AI systems as optimization problems to be attacked. It connects to prior work on decomposing complex behaviors into learnable components, drawing from hierarchical reinforcement learning and curriculum learning approaches. The use of synthetic simulation for adversarial training extends established practices in adversarial robustness research and safety testing. The paper responds to limitations in existing red teaming methodologies that often rely on hand-crafted attacks or unstructured adversarial probing, instead providing a more systematic and scalable approach to evaluation.
enables: This modular framework enables more thorough and systematic evaluation of AI system vulnerabilities across complex domains where sabotage requires coordinated multi-step behaviors. The approach opens pathways for automating red team attack design, reducing reliance on manual specification of adversarial strategies. By demonstrating how synthetic environments can strengthen real-world attacks, it sets up future work on scaling red team capabilities and continuously improving safety evaluations. The decomposition methodology could facilitate better understanding of which specific failure modes matter most in safety-critical applications, enabling more targeted defenses and more efficient allocation of safety research resources.
